author: @toml.8210
text:
You might calculate the horsepower using the voltage and current generated, but that doesn't account for system losses.  Your worm gear idea seems to be the best one, but doesn't eliminate friction loss in the gears.

Unfortunately, no matter how much you cover the fins, guides, or the turbines, water or moisture will get in, and will freeze in the cold winter, so it will need occasional cleaning and servicing.

Good on you;   Cheers.

author: @JehuMcSpooran
text: I was just thinking about this the other day. There are a few other Youtubers who have made their own arm style Torque meters. Another way I was thinking was more for power and energy measurement is to have the axle mounted parallel with the ground and tie a string with a 1kg weight to it. As the turbine spins, it coils the string and raises the weight. Time how far the weight rises in 1 minute or how long it takes to rise one metre and you can do the calcs from there.

author: @Digidoc316
text:
Torque multiplication through MA.  Worm drive is great for torque multiplication but there is a price of input speed versus output speed.
A 20:1 MA increases the torque by a 20/1 ratio but the speed is reduced by the inverse factor 1:20 
If the input to a 20:1 worm drive is 100 rpm, the output rpm =100/20 = 5 rpm.
However, if the input force is 25g, the output force is 20x25 = 500g.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Surface finish and lubrication are very much more important with worm gears because of tangential sidelateral drag will rob your system of a lot of force as well as rapidly grind out the gears.

I have had to replace a lot of such gears made of metal when they have been allowed to run out of lubrication because they are often at a lot more force and in a direction which favors the worm actually grinding the pinion away in a way that is far less pronounced with spur gears.

I would strongly advise sanding, then coating the gear surfaces to smooth them out, and then adding some oil or grease to ensure that the gears do not lose power due to friction, and if you plan on using it long term [if] then this will also help you to prevent them from destroying themselves quite so quickly in operation.

Of course, please do keep in mind that the viscosity of the lubricant works 2 ways, one is for you and one is against you.

The lighter, lower viscosity lubricants will reduce parasitic viscocetal drag, but will also, in the process allow more material friction and grinding...

The heavier, higher viscosity lubricants will increase viscocetal drag, but will also, in the process protect the material from friction and grinding.

It is best to find a happy medium based on your particular application.

For really small devices, 0 grade single weight motor oil is likely good enough.

For larger, stronger, higher loads, you may look into dedicated gear lubricants from GL-1 through GL-5 and for super heavy leading systems, move up to actual heavy wheel bearing and machinery grade grease.

Just keep in mind that in the real world, the whole reason for bearings is because things like to stick, grind, and ultimately stop working, without them.

The same is true for lubrication, especially where there is no way to make a bearing for your design.

author: @ZsOtherBrother
text:
Hi Robert, I feel like I'm missing the point here... since the worm gear can only be turned by the worm wheel, and all the ratios and measurements are known, (weight, wind speed, turbine "area", etc..), it seems to me that this mechanism can only determine if our wind turbine can supply the threshold torque required to lift the weight... can you please explain how this helps us measure the effectiveness?
Thank you

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: torque is all about effectiveness of the application of force - in a wind of a given speed over a given area then the available force is known - all you ave to do is hang weights on it and see if it can lift the calculated weight - equally you can see what it can lift and how different that is from what it should be able to lift

author: @fredericrike5974
text: Squirrel cage wheels are not known for being very efficient blowing air into the vanes and out the top- their main application is as a blower in many devices- with air pulled into the center and thrown out to the blades then exerting force on the vanes as they exit. Now that is in a "boxed unit" with air flows being controlled by the shape of the "box". But it has been around for centuries and no one seems much into using it either way to generate wind power- it wants a much higher wind velocity to even start for one thing. If it was easy, they wouldn't put a honkin" big capacitor on the motor to get started against that wheel. Nor has it the potential to gather and multiply the wind as your Darwin Wind Turbine does. But I'll watch if you care to explain to me how I've lost my way. Also the demonstrated results were dependent on not blowing directly into the center of the vanes, but toward the outer edged to put the wind directly into the vanes and not generally- or worse robbing the Wheel as the blades come around into the wind- so to be effective it will still need to be "canned" as they are conventionally used all over. Please don't take me wrong- I really want to understand this- I was an HVAC- air conditioning- technician and servicing engineer for 25 years- I have been using that type of fan for a long , long time- as a matter of fact, I probably have a dozen fan sections from four and five ton sir distribution systems. I have sold the larger ones I salvaged- they are quit pricey new- clean and repainted mine fetched half that and  never had one come back. But, I could see a string of those , say, over topping a fence line; five, ten or twenty on the same axle. But I don't think the efficiency is there to make it worthwhile.

author: @TrickyNekro
text:
Gear trains have loses though so you won't be able to convert all the torque, and if I remember right worm gears are some of the worst offenders. 

Funny enough they can be driven backwards, if angles / gear ratio is right enough, which also point to their lack of efficiency in a way.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: hmmm - actually mate they are stunning efficient if built well

replies.author: @TrickyNekro
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I've read somewhere that they are something like 60% efficient. I will check that again.

That they can be reverse driven is something I very amazingly found out myself as I also thought initially that they can not be reverse driven.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: we seem to be hearing different things mate - well that's often the way of it

replies.author: @TrickyNekro
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Nah, I've just checked, for worm drives with gear ratios between 1:10 - 1:60 efficiency is 85 - 40% respectively. 

Whether they can be backdriven or not depends on the lead angle. Very shallow and friction wins, with higher angles they will be back driven. Makes sense also lead screws are a form of wormgear and in most 3D printers you can lower the bed manually with a bit of care.
